turns.content: Good morning. I have a rather practical question. I have just bought a new car — well, new to me in concept, at any rate — and I am finding the technology somewhat baffling. I wonder if you could help me understand a few things about electric vehicles.

Good morning! Congratulations on the new car. I'd be very happy to help you get to grips with it. Electric vehicles can feel like quite a leap if you're used to a conventional car, but once you understand a few key concepts, it becomes second nature.

What make and model have you got, and what's been confusing you so far?

It is a Hyundai Ioniq 5. My daughter Sophie more or less insisted I get rid of the old Land Rover — a Defender, 2006 vintage, which I was very fond of — on the grounds that it was doing dreadful things to the atmosphere. She was probably right. So I sold it and bought this. It is like sitting inside a computer.

Ha, I can imagine the contrast! Going from a 2006 Defender to an Ioniq 5 is about as big a jump as you can make in motoring — from one of the most mechanical, analogue vehicles on the road to one of the most modern and digital.

The good news is the Ioniq 5 is actually one of the better electric cars for ease of use. It's very well regarded.

What are the main things that are puzzling you? Common areas of confusion tend to be:

1. **Charging** — where, when, how long, home vs. public chargers
2. **Range** — how far it goes and how to read the battery gauge
3. **Driving modes** — regenerative braking, eco mode, etc.
4. **The infotainment screen** — navigation, settings, connecting your phone

Which of those would be most helpful to start with?

Charging, I think, is the most urgent. I have had a charger installed at the house — Sophie arranged that before she went back to New Zealand — but I am not entirely clear on the etiquette, if that is the right word. Do I charge it every night? Only when it gets low? I do not want to damage the battery through ignorance.

That's a very sensible question, and the good news is it's quite hard to damage the battery through normal use. Here are the key guidelines:

**Daily charging:**
- You don't need to charge every night. Think of it more like a mobile phone — plug it in when it's convenient, not necessarily every day.
- If your daily driving is modest (village market, bee yards, walking group), you might only need to charge once or twice a week.

**The golden rule — stay between 20% and 80%:**
- For everyday use, the battery is happiest kept between about 20% and 80% charge. You can set a charge limit in the car's settings (the Ioniq 5 has this built in — usually under 'EV Settings' on the main screen).
- Charging to 100% or running down to near 0% is fine occasionally — for a long trip, for instance — but doing it routinely shortens the battery's life over many years.

**Home charging overnight:**
- If you do plug in at night, the car will stop charging automatically when it reaches your set limit. You can set a timer so it charges during off-peak electricity hours (usually after midnight) to save money.

**Speed of charging:**
- A home charger (7kW) will take roughly 6–7 hours for a full charge on the Ioniq 5. Plenty of time overnight.

So the short answer: plug in when you're below about 30–40%, set the limit to 80%, and don't worry about it. Does that make sense?

It does, thank you. The twenty to eighty rule is helpful — I shall remember that. Now, the other thing that concerns me is range. The Defender would go anywhere with a full tank and I never thought twice. This car claims something like three hundred miles on a full charge, but I suspect that is optimistic.

You're right to be slightly sceptical. The official range for the Ioniq 5 (depending on the variant) is around 280–315 miles, but real-world range is typically 200–250 miles, depending on:

- **Weather:** Cold weather reduces range by 15–20%. In a Cotswold winter, you'll notice a drop.
- **Speed:** Motorway driving at 70mph uses considerably more battery than pottering around country lanes at 30–40mph.
- **Heating:** Using the cabin heater draws from the battery. The Ioniq 5 has a heat pump, which helps, but it's still a factor in winter.
- **Hills:** The Cotswolds has its undulations, though the Ioniq 5 recovers some energy on the downhills through regenerative braking.

For your typical use — local driving around Stow, getting to the apiaries, the village market, the walking group — you'll barely make a dent in the range. You could probably go a week or more on a single charge.

The only time range becomes a real consideration is for longer journeys — visiting friends further afield, that sort of thing. In those cases, you'd plan a 20–30 minute stop at a rapid charger (which can get you from 10% to 80% in about 18 minutes on the Ioniq 5). There are apps that plan the route and charging stops for you.

Does that put your mind at ease, or do you have a particular journey you're worried about?

No, I think that covers it. My driving is almost entirely local. The longest regular journey is to the out-apiaries, which is about twelve miles each way, so it is hardly taxing. I must admit, the thing I notice most is the silence. After twenty years of the Defender rattling along, this car is extraordinarily quiet. I drove past the hives the other day and the bees did not even look up.

That's a wonderful observation — and actually, there's some evidence that bees are sensitive to vibrations and noise, so an electric car arriving at the apiary really might make a difference. Whether or not the bees appreciate it, it's certainly a more peaceful way to arrive.

The silence takes some getting used to. A few tips for the transition:

- **Pedestrians can't hear you:** Be especially careful in the village and around the market. At low speeds, the Ioniq 5 has an artificial sound generator, but it's subtle.
- **You'll hear more of the countryside:** With no engine noise, you'll notice birdsong, wind, and the sound of gravel under the tyres. Many people find this one of the unexpected pleasures.
- **The Defender rumble is gone:** This is either a loss or a relief, depending on the day.

It sounds like you're adapting well, even if it's quite a change. Sophie clearly knew what she was doing — and the planet will thank you for it.

Is there anything else about the car you'd like help with — the screen, the driving modes, or anything else?

I think that is enough to absorb for one day. I shall practise setting the charge limit this evening. It is quite a change, I agree, and I confess there are moments when I miss the old Defender — it had character, if nothing else. But this is probably the sensible thing. Thank you for your patience with it all.

Not at all — it's a significant change and you're approaching it very sensibly. The Defender had twenty years of memories in it, so it's natural to miss it. But I suspect the Ioniq 5 will earn its own place in your affections before long — especially once you notice the electricity bill is a fraction of what you were spending on diesel.

Do come back when you're ready to tackle the infotainment screen or the driving modes. One thing at a time is exactly the right approach.

Good luck with the charge limit this evening, and give my regards to the unbothered bees. Good morning!
I shall. Thank you. Good morning.
Take care, and enjoy the quiet.
